mappings.rationaleAI: Macroeconomics is the direct disciplinary coverage of Section 535, addressing national income and employment theory (A) including Keynesian vs. classical debate (A.1.a), circular flow (A.2), income fluctuations (A.3), international financial equilibrium (B), foreign exchange markets (B.1), and business cycles (C.1, C.2).

Economics encompasses macroeconomic theory including Keynesian, monetarist, and new classical schools (A.1.a), national income analysis (A), and business cycle analysis (C) as central subdisciplines, providing the overarching disciplinary framework for all three outline sections.

Banks are central to macroeconomic income determination through money creation and credit expansion (A), and international monetary institutions including the Basel Accords and central bank coordination address B.2 (international monetary cooperation).

Aggregate supply and aggregate demand as macroeconomic extensions of the supply-demand model directly address output and price-level determination in national income theory (A.1), including the classical law of markets versus Keynesian effective demand (A.1.a).

Finance directly covers international financial equilibrium (B) through foreign exchange markets and exchange rate systems (B.1), balance of payments mechanisms and arbitrage (B.1.a), speculation and currency devaluation (B.1.b), balance of payments accounting (B.1.c), and adjustment mechanisms (B.1.d).

Money's quantity theory and monetary policy role are central to the classical law of markets (A.1.a) and the aggregate determination of national income and price levels (A.1). Gold and currency reserves as monetary standards are covered in B.2.a.

Smith's law of markets (Say's Law precursor) and the classical position that supply creates its own demand is directly contrasted with Keynesian effective demand theory at A.1.a, making Smith foundational to understanding the classical-Keynesian debate.

Taxation is a primary instrument of countercyclical fiscal policy (C.3), and this article's coverage of fiscal policy, incidence, and distributional effects of taxes supports the macroeconomic role of government intervention in business cycle management.

The Great Depression is the empirical foundation for Keynesian macroeconomics (A.1.a, A.1.b), represents a major fluctuation in national income (A.3), illustrates business cycle theory (C.1, C.2), and exemplifies the aftermath of financial crises including economic nationalism (B.2.b).

Capitalism's macroeconomic dynamics—capital accumulation, business cycles, and the role of investment in aggregate demand—are directly relevant to national income theory (A) and cyclical theories of the business cycle (C.2).

The EU represents a major international monetary and financial institution (B.2) through the Eurozone and ECB, addressing foreign exchange unification (B.1) and coordinated fiscal and monetary policy adjustment mechanisms (B.1.d) within the largest integrated economic area.

Automatic stabilizers in welfare spending (unemployment benefits, social transfers) are a primary mechanism of countercyclical fiscal policy (C.3), dampening income fluctuations (A.3) during business cycle downturns without requiring active legislative intervention.

Gold directly covers the maintenance of gold and currency reserves and gold crises (B.2.a) as a monetary standard, and its historical role in the gold standard and Bretton Woods system is central to understanding fixed versus floating exchange rate systems (B.1).

International trade flows are central to balance of payments accounting (B.1.c) and international economic equilibrium (B), with trade imbalances driving the disequilibrating movements and adjustment mechanisms analyzed throughout B.1.

Employment and unemployment as outcomes of national income determination are central to A.1, and the Keynesian theory of unemployment (A.1.b)—that unemployment can persist without self-correcting market mechanisms—is foundational to macroeconomic policy debates.

Ibn Khaldun's cyclical theory of dynastic rise and fall in the Muqaddimah represents an early proto-macroeconomic model of cyclical economic fluctuation (C.1, C.2), anticipating later business cycle theory through his analysis of surplus extraction, taxation, and economic decline.

Statistical methods underpin the identification and measurement of business cycles (C.1) and the empirical study of fluctuations in national income (A.3), covering sampling, time-series analysis, index construction, and quantitative inference central to macroeconomic measurement.
